candidates.direction_name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Retest Three-CTA Residency There
ranking_notes: This is the best isolation experiment because it keeps the accepted compact PTX surface and imports only the one positive signal from the regressed run: higher CTA residency.
It directly matches the latest delta table: occupancy improved, but barrier and short-scoreboard regressed. This edit is designed to keep the first while avoiding the latter.
It is lower risk than another full sibling swap or a fresh tiling reopen.
candidates.hypothesis: The regular sibling regression shows that 3-CTA residency is not bad by itself; the bad part is the regular sibling's barrier-heavy consume path. Restoring the accepted compact PTX hot-band launch and then tightening only the PTX kernel launch-bounds target from 2 to 3 CTAs/SM is the cleanest way to test whether the occupancy win can be kept without inheriting the 10.15% barrier tax.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: The accepted compact PTX surface is still register-limited, but the beneficial part of the current regression may be the 3-CTA residency rather than the sibling kernel body.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is only a two-part local edit on the accepted PTX surface, but a tighter launch-bounds target can still introduce spills or weaken instruction scheduling.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again without a clear barrier reduction, or if build / NCU evidence suggests spill-driven residency forcing.
expected_local_changes: Restore the default fixed-shape hot-band dispatch to the compact PTX microkernel surface.
Retune the PTX hot-band launch-bounds annotation from the current 2-CTA target toward 3-CTA residency.
Leave grouped rows, shared-memory footprint, and stage depth unchanged in the first validation pass.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Trim Barrier Cadence Without Shared-Memory Blowup
ranking_notes: This is the strongest barrier-focused fallback once the regular sibling has been ruled out as a net win.
It stays below dir_01 because it is much riskier and touches the PTX microkernel loop directly.
It is still more aligned with the latest live bottleneck than a fresh tiling reopen.
candidates.hypothesis: The regular sibling regression moved the bottleneck from long-scoreboard to barrier plus short-scoreboard. A surgical edit on the accepted compact PTX surface that trims wait/sync cadence without adding the extra x32 shared-memory staging buffers could recover the barrier loss while preserving the compact row-pair microkernel's better long-scoreboard and mio-throttle behavior.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Single-K barrier cadence and CTA handoff overhead on the accepted compact PTX hot-band surface remain the unresolved latency tax once the row-pair live-range reset is in place.
candidates.risk: High. This is still PTX-microkernel surgery, and it can easily break correctness or recreate the staged-family regressions if the shared-memory footprint or synchronization contract grows.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory rises above the accepted 22,016 B footprint or if barrier does not improve materially against the accepted PTX base.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted compact PTX microkernel launch surface before editing the wait/sync cadence.
Trim the single-K consumer barrier / wait-group handoff without allocating a second full staged buffer pair.
Keep grouped rows and hot-band geometry unchanged while testing the synchronization change.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Try Grouped Rows Equals Two
ranking_notes: This stays in the set as the cheapest opposite-direction launch-order test after the earlier grouped_rows=8 loss.
It ranks below dir_02 because the current live regression is more clearly about barrier cadence than grouped traversal.
Use it only after the PTX launch-bounds isolation and barrier-trim probes fail or plateau.
candidates.hypothesis: The earlier grouped_rows=8 test already showed that deeper B reuse was the wrong launch-order direction. After the current run demonstrated that occupancy alone is insufficient, the cheapest remaining launch-order probe is to restore the compact PTX base and reduce kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 2, favoring tighter A locality and shorter grouped traversal without changing stage depth or the consume kernel body.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: The accepted PTX grouped-row traversal may still be mismatched to the A/B locality balance on the fixed hot-band surface.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is a very small change, but the upside may be noise-sized because the opposite grouped-row direction already failed and launch order was never the main bottleneck.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within noise or if barrier / scoreboard behavior does not improve after one measurement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted compact PTX hot-band dispatch if needed.
Leave launch-bounds, stage depth, and the hot-band consume helper unchanged for the first measurement.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
